does: This paper introduces abstractive red-teaming, a systematic method for discovering natural-language categories of user queries that reliably cause language models to violate their intended character or values. Rather than manually crafting adversarial examples, the approach searches for generalizable patterns of prompts that expose character failures. The method abstracts away from individual adversarial instances to find broader classes of inputs that consistently trigger unwanted model behaviors, enabling researchers to identify vulnerabilities in model alignment and character before deployment.
connects: This work builds on the growing literature of red-teaming and adversarial testing for large language models, extending earlier approaches that relied on manual enumeration of edge cases or simple perturbation-based adversarial examples. It responds to the challenge of scaling safety evaluation beyond hand-crafted test cases, connecting to research on jailbreaking, prompt injection, and character alignment failure modes. The abstractive approach relates to work on discovering interpretable adversarial patterns and systematic evaluation frameworks that move beyond isolated examples toward understanding classes of problematic inputs.
enables: This research enables more comprehensive pre-deployment safety auditing by providing a method to systematically discover novel failure modes at scale. It points toward automated characterization of model vulnerabilities and could inform development of more robust character training procedures. The framework for abstracting adversarial patterns into natural-language categories could extend to other model behaviors and values, supporting the creation of better safety benchmarks, improved training objectives that account for discovered failure classes, and more targeted defenses against character violations.
